title: UC2 — A new mission-control replica won't land on the launch pad
description: Mission control needs a replacement replica on the Artemis launch pad. When the new pod won't surface, hand the diagnosis to artemis-launch-pad-debugger — the agent crosses resource boundaries you would have walked by hand. (Tour copy will be re-narrated under the mission framing convention by STORY-032.)

steps.title: Apply the broken state
steps.explanation:
A replacement `mission-control` replica needs to come up, but the launch pad has been declared unsafe — a synthetic node taint blocks scheduling. Apply the manifests below to your vCluster. The five files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`), then applied with a single `kubectl apply -f`.

The applied bundle is: a namespace, RBAC for the bootstrap Job, the bootstrap Job itself (which taints the only node and forces a rollout restart), a Service, and the broken Deployment. The Deployment uses the **real** `mission-control:v1` image — only the scheduling constraint is unsatisfiable.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
    kagent-workshop/scenario: pod-pending

# RBAC for the bootstrap-launch-pad-fault Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml).
#
# The Job needs two cluster-scoped + namespaced permissions:
#   - patch nodes  → to apply the synthetic taint via `kubectl taint`
#   - patch deploy → to `kubectl rollout restart` mission-control after the taint,
#                    which closes the cold-deploy race that would otherwise let a
#                    Pod bind to the node before the taint lands (NFR-003).
#
# Both permissions are scoped to the absolute minimum verbs required.
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc2-bootstrap-node-tainter
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["nodes"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc2-bootstrap-node-tainter
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: artemis-uc2-bootstrap-node-tainter
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
    namespace: artemis-uc2
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
    namespace: artemis-uc2

# bootstrap-launch-pad-fault — applies the synthetic node taint that blocks
# mission-control from scheduling, then forces a rollout restart so the Pod
# is re-evaluated against the now-tainted node (closes the cold-deploy race
# where the scheduler binds the Pod before the taint lands — NFR-003).
#
# Idempotency:
#   - `kubectl taint --overwrite` is idempotent on identical key=value:effect.
#   - `kubectl rollout restart` always succeeds; if no Pod is currently scheduled
#     it just bumps the Deployment's pod template hash, producing a new Pending
#     Pod that hits the taint deterministically.
#   - The Job itself is named, not generateName'd; re-applying the same manifest
#     is a no-op ("unchanged"). To re-run, delete the Job first.
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  backoffLimit: 4
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 120
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 600
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
        app: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: bootstrap-launch-pad-fault
      restartPolicy: OnFailure
      containers:
        - name: tainter
          image: docker.io/bitnami/kubectl:1.31.0
          command: ["/bin/bash", "-eu", "-o", "pipefail", "-c"]
          args:
            - |
              echo "[bootstrap] tainting all nodes with artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule"
              kubectl taint nodes --all artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule --overwrite

              echo "[bootstrap] waiting up to 60s for deployment/mission-control to exist in artemis-uc2"
              for i in $(seq 1 60); do
                if kubectl get -n artemis-uc2 deploy/mission-control >/dev/null 2>&1; then
                  echo "[bootstrap] deployment found after ${i}s"
                  break
                fi
                sleep 1
              done

              if ! kubectl get -n artemis-uc2 deploy/mission-control >/dev/null 2>&1; then
                echo "[bootstrap] deployment did not appear within 60s — taint applied; exiting"
                exit 0
              fi

              echo "[bootstrap] forcing rollout restart of mission-control to surface the untolerated-taint event"
              kubectl rollout restart -n artemis-uc2 deploy/mission-control

              echo "[bootstrap] done"
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "32Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control
    spec:
      # Deliberately no toleration for artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault — the
      # bootstrap Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml) applies that taint with
      # NoSchedule effect, leaving the Pod Pending with an "untolerated taint"
      # event (FR-010). The image tag :v1 is real (the same one UC4's
      # mission-control-pending Deployment will reuse) — only the scheduling
      # constraint is unsatisfiable, per FR-010 AC.
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          image: ghcr.io/kagent-workshop/mission-control:v1
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

steps.title: CLI baseline — first look at the pod
steps.explanation:
Give the bootstrap Job ~30 s to taint the node and bump the Deployment, then start where any on-call would: the workload's own pods. Run the three commands below in order.

Notice how each one tells you that **something** is wrong but none of them name the cause. The pod is `Pending`. Its events mention scheduling, but the Pod itself doesn't carry the taint — the **node** does. You'll have to switch resources to find it.

steps.title: CLI baseline — now look at the node
steps.explanation:
The Pod is Pending and the scheduler emitted `FailedScheduling: 0/1 nodes are available: 1 node(s) had untolerated taint`. To name the taint, switch to the node side. The taint key, value, and effect are all under the `Taints:` section of `describe node`.

You now have the answer — but you assembled it yourself, by hand, from two resource kinds. That's the friction the agent is about to remove.

commands.label: Describe the node (look at Taints:)
commands.command: kubectl describe node $(kubectl get nodes -o name | head -n1)

steps.title: Now ask the agent
steps.explanation:
Hand the same problem to **`artemis-launch-pad-debugger`**. The agent has the same Kubernetes read tools you used above — `get/describe pod`, `get/describe node`, `get events` — the difference is that it correlates them across the two resource kinds in a single synthesis.

The agent runs in the `artemis-uc2` namespace and uses the participant-scoped `artemis-llm` ModelConfig.

commands.label: Ask the agent
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-launch-pad-debugger --namespace artemis-uc2 --task 'The mission-control pod in the artemis-uc2 namespace is stuck Pending. Diagnose it.'

steps.title: What did the agent do better?
steps.explanation:
In the CLI baseline you held two pieces of state from two resource kinds — a Pending Pod with a `FailedScheduling` event, and a Node with an `artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule` taint — and joined them yourself. The agent ran the same tools across both resources and returned a single sentence: **the only node carries a synthetic `artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule` taint, which the `mission-control` Pod does not tolerate**.

This is one notch above UC1's value: same idea (one synthesis instead of N commands), but now the synthesis crosses a resource boundary the participant had to cross by hand. UC3 will scale this further to external observability; UC4 to multi-agent fan-out.
